questions.question: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. What is the probability the second marble is blue given the first was red?
questions.explanation: After drawing a red marble, the bag has 4 red and 3 blue — 7 total. $P(\text{2nd blue} \mid \text{1st red}) = 3/7$.

questions.question: If you roll a standard die and are told the result is an even number, what is the probability that you rolled a 6?
questions.explanation: The given set is even numbers: {2, 4, 6} — 3 outcomes. Only one is a 6. $P = 1/3$.

questions.question: A card is drawn from a 52-card deck. Given the card is a Spade, what is the probability it is an Ace?
questions.explanation: There are 13 Spades (the new sample space). Only 1 is an Ace. $P = 1/13$.

questions.question: In a class of 30 students, 10 play soccer, 12 play basketball, and 4 play both. Given a student plays basketball, what is the probability they also play soccer?
questions.explanation: The given is the 12 basketball players. 4 of those also play soccer. $4/12 = 1/3$.

questions.question: A survey of 100 people: 60 like coffee, 50 like tea, and 30 like both. Given a person likes coffee, what is the probability they also like tea?
questions.explanation: Of the 60 coffee drinkers, 30 also like tea. $P = 30/60 = 1/2$.

questions.question: Two dice are rolled. Given that the sum is 8, what is the probability that one of the dice shows a 5?
Two coins are flipped. Given that at least one is Heads, what is the probability that both are Heads?
questions.explanation: Given at least one H: {HH, HT, TH} — 3 outcomes. Only {HH} qualifies. $P = 1/3$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You understand how new information changes the odds — conditional probability with cards, dice, marbles, survey data, and using the formula $P(A|B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Key reminders: (1) $P(A|B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ — the given event goes in the denominator, (2) The given event restricts the sample space, (3) $P(A \cap B) = P(B|A) \cdot P(A)$, (4) $P(A|B)$ is not generally equal to $P(B|A)$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: (1) Identifying the new sample space after the condition is applied, (2) Using the formula correctly — don't swap numerator and denominator, (3) For 'without replacement' problems, adjust the counts after each draw, (4) Conditional probability is not symmetric.
scoreMessages.0: Review conditional probability: (1) $P(B|A)$ means the probability of B given that A has occurred, (2) $P(B|A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$ — divide the overlap by the given, (3) The 'given' restricts the sample space to only those outcomes, (4) For 'without replacement' problems, update the counts after each selection.
